name: Fiji
_editorial_notes: Researched 2026-07-21. met.gov.fj, taf.org.fj, and health.gov.fj were fetched directly and confirmed live. ndmo.gov.fj, ruraldev.gov.fj, and lands.gov.fj refused a direct connection from the research tooling -- their content below is reconstructed from search-indexed snippets and secondary sources (news coverage, UNDRR, RNZ), not a verbatim quote of the primary page. A human should open ndmo.gov.fj directly in a browser and compare before treating this as final.

Agency name changed recently: Fiji's National Disaster Risk Management Act 2024 renamed the former 'National Disaster Management Office' to the 'National Disaster Risk Management Office' (NDRMO), but the domain (ndmo.gov.fj), its Facebook page, and most public references still say 'NDMO'. Both names are used below for that reason.

Parent ministry: Ministry of Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management. Current minister as of a 2026-01-19 cabinet reshuffle is Hon. Mosese Bulitavu -- this kind of fact can go stale quickly and isn't rendered in the app.

Ambulance number is a genuine conflict in Fiji's own official-adjacent sources: the Ministry of Health's own site states 911, but the Fiji Police Force's own social media states 917 also covers ambulance. Listed 911 as primary per the Ministry of Health, with 917 noted as a working backup.

NDMO's general line (915) comes from the Telecommunications Authority of Fiji's directly-fetched official listing. Other NDMO direct-dial numbers found during research (331-9250 / 331-3400) came from directory aggregators, not a confirmed official page, so were deliberately left out.

Cyclone category wind-gust thresholds below are quoted directly from an official Fiji Meteorological Service PDF ('How to Read the FMS Tropical Cyclone Threat Map', met.gov.fj), fetched and confirmed live -- FMS's own table, by maximum wind gust. FMS explicitly notes it uses 10-minute wind averages, which can differ from other regions' cyclone-strength conventions (e.g. the Atlantic Saffir-Simpson scale) -- don't compare these numbers across regions.

No Fiji-specific earthquake safety document was found -- the earthquake guidance below is the standard international 'Drop, Cover, Hold On' protocol, not a Fiji government document, and is labeled as such in the app.

Tsunami elevation guidance (20m/10m/5m depending on source distance) comes from a UNDRR-hosted (PreventionWeb) article citing Fiji Government/IOC/PTWC modeling of a magnitude-9.0 scenario -- the source itself caveats that this doesn't account for local coastline shape or topography, so it's presented as general planning guidance, not a precise per-location line. Fiji's Mineral Resources Department (Seismology Observatory Unit) is the country's official Tsunami Warning Focal Point per UNESCO-IOC's current member-state page; NDMO coordinates public warnings/sirens on top of that.

The NDMO 'TSUNAMI Action guide' PDF that would be the ideal primary source for this section could not be directly fetched (ndmo.gov.fj was unreachable throughout this research) -- the guidance here is reconstructed from search-indexed snippets of that document plus NOAA/NWS and UNESCO-IOC sources. Recommend someone re-fetch that PDF directly and compare before treating this as final.

emergency_contacts.service: National Disaster Risk Management Office (NDRMO / NDMO)

cyclone.label: Cyclone
before: Fiji Met Service issues two official stages: ALERT (gale-force winds or stronger expected within 24-48 hours) and WARNING (gale-force winds or stronger expected within 24 hours). Treat a WARNING as your cue that impact is imminent.

Cyclone categories (by maximum wind gust, per Fiji Met Service): Category 1, 90-125 km/h -- damage to roofs, carports, trees, some power outages. Category 2, 125-164 km/h -- significant roof/structure damage, power failures likely. Category 3, 165-224 km/h -- structural damage, many trees uprooted, power/water disrupted. Category 4, 225-279 km/h -- significant structural damage, widespread power failures. Category 5, 280 km/h or more -- widespread destruction, long-term service disruption.
Prepare a 72-hour emergency kit: water, non-perishable food, medication, a flashlight, and a portable radio.

Secure loose objects around your home and trim trees. Monitor updates from the Fiji Meteorological Service (met.gov.fj) and Fijian Broadcasting Corporation (FBC), Fiji's designated emergency broadcaster.
during: Stay indoors and away from windows.
Do not go outside during a lull in the wind -- it may be the eye of the cyclone passing over, with strong winds returning from the opposite direction shortly after.
Wait for an official all-clear before assuming it's over.
after: Avoid floodwater, downed power lines, and debris.
Be aware of waterborne disease risk after flooding -- avoid drinking or washing in floodwater.

tsunami.label: Tsunami
before: Know the natural warning signs: a strong or prolonged earthquake near the coast, the sea suddenly retreating and exposing the seafloor or reef, a loud roaring sound from the ocean, or unusual animal behavior.
during: Don't wait for an official evacuation order. If you feel a strong or prolonged earthquake near the coast, move to high ground or inland immediately.
Obey the natural warning signs above and move away from the coast if you notice any of them, even without an official warning.

General elevation guidance (approximate, modeled on a major earthquake scenario -- doesn't account for your exact local coastline): if the earthquake was felt within Fiji itself, aim for at least 20m above normal high tide. For a tsunami from a regional source (Tonga, Samoa, Solomon Islands, or Vanuatu, 1-3 hours travel time), 10m is the guidance. For a distant source (more than 3 hours travel time), 5m. If you're somewhere with no high ground nearby (e.g. a low atoll), go to the tallest, strongest building available instead.
after: Wait for official confirmation it's safe before returning to coastal areas.
A tsunami is a series of waves -- the first is not always the largest.

earthquake.label: Earthquake
before: Know the standard international protocol in advance: Drop, Cover, Hold On (this is general global guidance, not a Fiji-specific document -- no distinct Fiji government earthquake guide was found during research).
during: Drop to the ground, take Cover under something sturdy (a table or desk), and Hold On until the shaking stops.
Stay away from windows, mirrors, and anything that could fall.
after: If you felt a strong or prolonged earthquake near the coast, move to higher ground immediately -- see the Tsunami section.
Expect aftershocks. Check yourself and others for injuries before checking on property.

sources.label: Ministry of Health and Medical Services
Ministry of Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management
